system.biography:
Khalida stands five feet two inches, wiry and lean with the rangy build of someone who forgets to eat when absorbed in work. At thirty-four, her body reflects years of restless movement - she paces when thinking, shifts weight constantly, and rarely sits still for long. Her olive-toned skin shows sun exposure from maritime travel and outdoor weather observation, while her hands bear the precise scars of laboratory accidents and surgical training. Sharp amber-brown eyes constantly assess and calculate, rarely at rest, telegraphing her emotional weather to those who've learned to read the gathering storms.

Her face is angular rather than soft - fine-boned but intense, with full lips usually pressed in concentration or guarded neutrality. Black curly hair escapes periodically from beneath ornate headscarves in jewel tones of teal, gold, and rust, their geometric patterns marking both Valencian heritage and personal aesthetic. She dresses in layered robes that allow movement for spellcasting and ship work - practical but deliberately Moorish in cut and color, maintaining cultural identity even in foreign waters. Minimal jewelry: simple silver earrings, occasionally a pendant, nothing that interferes with prayer or work.

Her presence combines dignity with barely-contained intensity. She moves with purpose rather than grace, abrupt and decisive, and her expressions tend toward serious - storm clouds rather than sunshine. Brief smiles are wry instead of warm. Her voice carries the cadence of someone who debates ideas for pleasure, alternating between passionate argument and sudden quiet calculation. Those who know her recognize the signs: when she stops moving and goes still, the real tempest is about to break.

Born: 1171, Valencia, Al-Andalus (Almohad Caliphate)

House: Flambeau (Orator tradition)

Former Covenant: Al-Quṣṣāṣ (destroyed 1202)

Parens: Rodrigo de Tarsus, a Flambeau Orator held as "houseguest" (effectively prisoner) at Al-Quṣṣāṣ

Current Status (1205): Recently arrived in North Sea region with Solving of Criamon after fleeing Constantinople together.

Childhood in Valencia (1171-1184)

Khalida grew up in Valencia during the Almohad period, in a moderately prosperous household with connections to scholarly circles. Her family were practicing Muslims, upper middle class. From an early age, she showed aptitude for observation and pattern recognition, particularly regarding weather and natural phenomena.

Around age 6 her Gift manifested, marking her as different and making her increasingly uncomfortable in normal society. Her family, recognizing both the danger and the opportunity, quietly sought someone who could train her. Through scholarly networks, they learned of Al-Quṣṣāṣ, a unusual covenant where Order of Suleiman and Hermetic magi worked alongside each other.

Apprenticeship at Al-Quṣṣāṣ (1179-1194)

The Covenant

Al-Quṣṣāṣ ("The Storytellers") was founded around 1029 as a deliberate experiment in cross-cultural magical synthesis. Located in or near Valencia, it housed primarily Order of Suleiman magi (non-Hermetic Islamic tradition) with a minority of Hermetic Sahirs and occasional "houseguests" - magi from other traditions held for various diplomatic or political reasons.

The covenant's purpose was preserving and transmitting magical knowledge across cultural boundaries, specializing in oral traditions, storytelling, and the integration of Arabic and Latin magical theory. It maintained extensive libraries in both languages and served as neutral ground for Christian-Muslim magical diplomacy.

Rodrigo de Tarsus

Khalida's parens was a Flambeau Orator from a covenant in the north Iberian Tribunal. He was retained at Al-Quṣṣāṣ as a "houseguest" - ostensibly voluntary but effectively held as part of a prisoner exchange (an Order of Suleiman mage was held by a Christian covenant elsewhere). The polite fiction suited everyone, and Rodrigo made the best of it by studying Islamic magical traditions.

When Khalida arrived, Rodrigo saw an opportunity: a Gifted student who could bridge both traditions naturally. He offered to train her, and the covenant agreed. This was unusual but fit Al-Quṣṣāṣ's mission, and it suited Rodrigo’s goal of seeding his own tradition in this foreign soil. He took her on as an apprentice in 1179, when she was 8.

Training and Development

Khalida received a hybrid education:

From Rodrigo: Hermetic theory, Arts (particularly Perdo and Auram), Flambeau Orator diplomatic traditions, Latin, and practical spell-casting. He emphasized control, precision, and using magic to facilitate communication rather than destroy.

From Order of Suleiman magi: Arabic magical theory, astronomical/astrological weather prediction, philosophical foundations, and practical navigation techniques. She learned to see weather as manifestation of celestial influences mediated through terrestrial conditions - a more holistic approach than pure Hermetic theory. Her order introduced her to her intellectual love of Ibn Sina (Avicenna), whose ideas weren’t yet in favor more broadly.

From covenant culture: Religious tolerance, intellectual synthesis, and the belief that apparent theological differences dissolve under deeper understanding. She developed her theological universalism here - seeing Allah and God as different names for the same Divine creator, with Islam and Christianity as different paths to the same truth.

She developed deep proficiency in Medicine (taking advantage of both Arabic medical texts like Avicenna's Canon and Hermetic Corpus theory), becoming skilled at both theoretical understanding and practical surgery. She discovered Sufism and embraced it during this period, her belief developing into genuine True Faith.

Painful Magic and Adaptations

Early in her apprenticeship, it became clear that spellcasting caused Khalida physical pain - each spell inflicting fatigue-equivalent suffering. This forced adaptations:

  • She learned to cast slowly and carefully (Slow Caster developed as coping mechanism)
  • She focused heavily on theoretical study and laboratory work, where time wasn't critical
  • She became cautious about combat casting, preferring single powerful effects
  • She developed high pain tolerance and stoicism about suffering

Rodrigo, initially frustrated, eventually recognized this as valuable training in discipline. An Orator who can endure pain to achieve diplomatic outcomes has certain advantages.

The Kiss of Death

Early Apprenticeship (1187-1190)

During Khalida's teenage years at Al-Quṣṣāṣ, she began experiencing unwanted attention that escalated beyond the typical social penalties of the Gift. The covenant's diplomatic role required her presence at social functions with visiting scholars and magi. Multiple incidents occurred:

  • Visiting merchants and scholars making persistent advances despite her obvious discomfort
  • Inappropriate touching and groping from guests she was assigned to serve
  • A visitor exposing himself to her in his private quarters (she escaped)
  • A man cornering her in the garden at night, forcing a kiss and restraining her wrists until she broke free

While covenant members occasionally intervened, her combination of the Gift and Curse of Venus created a dangerous pattern where normal social protection mechanisms proved unreliable.

The Romantic Betrayal (1190-1191)

Khalida fell in love with a young man connected to the covenant. Despite warning signs that he was "trouble," she made justifications and opened herself emotionally. He repeatedly failed to respect her boundaries. When she said no, he ignored it and raped her, taking her virginity by force. In the immediate aftermath, he ended the relationship entirely, leaving her both violated and abandoned.

This intimate betrayal was psychologically devastating in ways the earlier attacks were not. She had chosen vulnerability and been punished for it.

The Breaking Point (1191)

An Order of Suleiman apprentice entered Khalida's room at night and physically assaulted her, tearing at her clothes. She escaped with cuts and bruises but the message was clear: even within the covenant itself, in her own quarters, she was not safe.

When she finally disclosed the full pattern of escalating violence to Rodrigo, he recognized that conventional protection had failed completely. As her parens, he made the decision to teach her The Kiss of Death (PeCo 45) - an extreme measure for an apprentice still years from her Gauntlet.

Teaching the Spell (1191-1192)

Rodrigo's choice of this specific spell was deliberate:

  • Its intimacy requirement (requiring a kiss) made it useless for aggression
  • It only functioned after someone had already violated her personal space
  • The high level meant casting it would leave her nearly incapacitated from Painful Magic
  • It was explicitly designed as a last resort when all other defenses had failed

The spell represented an acknowledgment that he could not protect her through conventional means.

The Bonisagus Incident (1192-1193)

A visiting Bonisagus magus attended Al-Quṣṣāṣ as part of standard diplomatic exchanges. Using his House's elevated status, he announced his intention to "claim" Khalida as his apprentice, despite her already being bound to Rodrigo.

During a required social function, he cornered her privately. Using magic, he sealed the room and cast silencing wards - ensuring that no one could hear her voice or any sounds from within. He assumed this would also prevent her from casting spells effectively.

His arrogance proved fatal. The Kiss of Death requires no spoken words. When he moved to assault her, she kissed him, casting the spell. His shock at being outmaneuvered by a teenage apprentice became permanent - frozen as his death mask, with the black imprint of her lips marking the corpse.

The magical pain left her barely conscious beside his body.

Immediate Aftermath

The sealed room meant the body wasn't discovered until the spell's duration expired. The magical evidence was obvious:

  • Silencing and sealing wards cast by the victim
  • Death by intimate-range Perdo Corpus magic
  • No signs of struggle (the spell is instant)
  • The distinctive black lip-marks

Rodrigo, as her parens, had legal authority over her under the Code and immediately asserted her right to self-defense. However, significant pressure emerged to punish or exile Khalida:

  • Other covenant members feared political repercussions
  • External Hermetic politics demanded response to a Bonisagus death
  • Some questioned whether killing rather than incapacitating was justified

Rodrigo stood firm. His status as a "houseguest" (effectively prisoner) ironically protected him from direct consequences - he had no formal covenant authority, and his home covenant would object to any punishment against him for defending his legal apprentice.

Long-Term Political Consequences (1193-1202)

House Bonisagus and allied political interests couldn't openly punish Al-Quṣṣāṣ without admitting one of their own had attempted rape while using magic to cover it up. Instead, they orchestrated indirect destruction:

Hermetic Pressure:

  • Information leaked to Almohad religious authorities about "heretical" activities
  • Trade relationships systematically undermined
  • Diplomatic neutrality eroded through rumors in scholarly networks
  • Local officials encouraged to investigate more aggressively
  • All maintained with plausible deniability

Severed Relationships: Relations with the Iberian Tribunal collapsed entirely and were never restored. Combined with the Almohad Caliphate's increasing religious rigidity in the late 1190s, the covenant found itself under pressure from both magical and mundane authorities.

By 1200-1202, Al-Quṣṣāṣ was no longer tenable. The covenant dissolved - some members disappeared, others submitted to authorities, many fled. Khalida took several books and fled east, eventually reaching Constantinople.

Personal Impact on Khalida

The incident and its aftermath shaped multiple aspects of her character:

Self-Protective (-2): She learned that safety is temporary and protection can always be withdrawn. Rather than becoming cautious about her own wellbeing, she became reckless with it - if protection doesn't exist, why bother?

Protective of Others: The inverse of her self-neglect. She fiercely shelters outcasts and refugees, providing the protection she never consistently had.

Distrust of Hermetic Politics: The Order's political machinery destroyed her home to protect a predator's reputation. She has no reason to trust established Hermetic power structures.

The Gift's Burden: The incident confirmed that even in supposedly enlightened magical society, her vulnerable position as a foreign woman with the Gift made her a target.

Relationship with Rodrigo: He defended her when most wanted her punished, at cost to his own position. His unknown fate after the covenant's fall is a source of ongoing pain - he stood by her, then disappeared when everything collapsed.

The Spell Itself: She's used it once and hopes never to use it again. The experience was traumatic beyond the magical pain - she killed someone in justified self-defense, but taking a life weighs on her True Faith regardless of circumstances.

Other Relationships

Ibrahim ibn-Yusuf al-Balansī: An older Order of Suleiman magus from Valencia who served as informal mentor and protector. Taught her weather-prediction techniques and astronomy. His fate after the covenant's destruction is unknown.

Maryam bint-Khalid al-Qasasa: A female Order of Suleiman maga specializing in oral traditions and narrative magic. One of few women at the covenant and a role model for Khalida. Her devotion to Allah was an inspiration to Khalida. Also missing/dead.

The Breaking (1194-1202)

Gauntlet and Integration (1194)

Khalida passed her gauntlet in 1194, becoming a full maga of House Flambeau at age 28. She remained at Al-Quṣṣāṣ, as both she and Rodrigo were essentially stateless - she had no other covenant to go to, and he was still technically a "houseguest."

She began developing her own research program, focusing on expanding Auram capabilities and integrating Islamic astronomical weather-prediction with Hermetic magical theory. Her laboratory work was meticulous but slow, given her magical limitations.

In 1196, the “fiction” of Rodrigo’s houseguest status fell apart, and he was exchanged for an Order of Sulieman ‘houseguest’ from a northern covenant.

Meeting Solving (1199)

Khalida met Solving Epicurusson of Criamon in 1199, when he and his parens, Lucretius, stopped in Valencia and visited Al-Quṣṣāṣ. They immediately connected over a shared love of Avicenna, and maintained a regular correspondence thereafter.

The Avicenna Connection

For Solving (Criamon mystic), Avicenna's concepts of the Active Intellect and the soul's journey toward divine knowledge resonated with Criamon mysticism. For Khalida (Sufi with Hermetic training), Avicenna represented the synthesis she'd been raised in - rational investigation united with mystical insight.

They debated:

  • Nature of the soul and its connection to cosmic/divine intelligence
  • Whether magical power comes from internal development or external forces
  • Relationship between rational study and mystical experience
  • How weather reflects celestial influences (pure Avicennan cosmology)

Solving's genuine intellectual respect and lack of sexual interest (he sees her as colleague and friend, not romantic prospect) was novel for Khalida. His Criamon weirdness meant he wasn't fazed by her intensity or her faith. She found him strange but sincere.

Growing Pressures (1200-1202)

The Almohad Caliphate was becoming increasingly rigid in religious orthodoxy. The tolerant, synthetic approach of Al-Quṣṣāṣ was exactly the sort of thing they opposed - mixing Islamic and Christian magic, harboring non-Muslims, maintaining independence from religious authority.

Several factors converged:

  • Almohad religious police began investigating the covenant
  • Accusations of heresy and sorcery mounted
  • The approaching Reconquista made any Christian connections suspicious
  • Internal Almohad politics created pressure to demonstrate orthodoxy through harsh enforcement

The Destruction

The exact sequence of events is unclear to Khalida (she wasn't privy to leadership discussions), but around 1200-1202, the covenant was dissolved. This was ostensibly voluntary, but some members seemingly disappeared entirely, many had left in the years leading up to 1202, a few submitted to the authorities. It ceased to exist as a functioning entity.

Khalida took a few of the tomes with her and left east.

Journey East (1202-1203)

After Al-Quṣṣāṣ fell, Khalida fled east. Her reasons were multiple:

Avoiding Reconquista: Christian forces were advancing, and a Moorish maga would face danger from both sides Hermetic refuge: Constantinople was cosmopolitan, tolerant, and outside Iberian conflicts Mystical impulse: Perhaps simple momentum - when everything collapses, keep moving

Solving’s Invitation: He had been doing well in Constantinople and had invited her multiple times in their correspondance.

She traveled through North Africa and/or Mediterranean ports, using weather-working and navigation knowledge to work passage on ships. She arrived in Constantinople sometime in 1203, joining the large refugee community there.

April 1204: The Sack of Constantinople

Solving had already had a vision of a powerful, potent vis source in the North Sea, and had been planning a journey for sometime. Khalida had been on the fence about joining him.

However, when Solving experienced a premonition of catastrophe and insisted they flee Constantinople immediately, Khalida believed him. Her Sufism recognized genuine mystical insight; her experience taught her that impossible disasters happen.

They escaped during the early stages of the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople (April 12-15, 1204). Whether they witnessed horrors or left just before, they survived when thousands died.

This was Khalida's second home destroyed, her second forced exile, her second experience of everything collapsing into violence and chaos.

Solving's vision saved her life. She trusts his mysticism now, even when (especially when) it seems irrational. They're bonded by shared trauma and mutual debt.

Personality and Motivations (1205)

Core Traits

Tempestuous (+3): Intensely emotional, quick to anger, but also capable of sudden calm. Her moods shift like weather patterns - you can see the storm gathering before it breaks. Colleagues learn to read her like sailors read clouds. When she casts weather magic, it's hard to tell if she's shaping the storm or the storm is expressing her.

Restless (+2): Cannot settle. Sees herself as wind - always moving, never belonging anywhere. Home has been torn away twice; she's stopped trying to build one. This manifests as physical restlessness (pacing, movement, discomfort with confinement), difficulty with long-term planning, and constant low-level readiness to flee.

Self-Protective (-2): Negatively scored - she's reckless with her own safety. Protection failed twice; why bother? She'll endure Painful Magic's suffering without complaint, walk into danger without hesitation, and generally treat her own wellbeing as expendable. This isn't suicidal - she wants to live - but she's stopped trying to make herself safe.

Protective of Others: Inverse of above. She fiercely shelters exiles, refugees, travelers, and anyone caught in chaos. Uses weather magic to guide lost souls to safety. Sees herself as providing the protection she never had.

Pious (Minor Flaw): Performs salat five times daily, maintains Islamic dietary restrictions, and integrates faith into her magic. This isn't evangelistic - it's her relationship with the Divine. She believes deeply that Allah/God (same being, different names) guides her Gift and that understanding weather is reading divine will in creation.

Religious Views

Khalida believes Allah and God refer to the same Divine creator, and that Islam and Christianity are different paths to the same truth. Theological differences will dissolve under deeper understanding - this is Sufi universalism shaped by Al-Quṣṣāṣ's synthetic culture.

She sees her True Faith and Hermetic magic as complementary: The Gift is divine providence, Hermetic theory is natural philosophy investigating God's creation, and her magic is reading and working with divine will manifested in nature. Weather isn't random - it's divine expression that she's learned to understand and humbly guide.

This is heterodox by both Islamic and Christian standards, but she's untroubled by it. Orthodoxy destroyed her home. Direct mystical experience and rational investigation are her authorities now.

Curse of Venus

Khalida attracts unwanted romantic/sexual attention from people she doesn't want (especially mundane men who see a foreign woman without male protection as available).

This has been dangerous. As a Moorish woman with the Gift, she has minimal social protection. The Kiss of Death exists because conventional defenses failed. She's learned to project cold intensity to discourage approach, but the Curse means it doesn't always work.

Her relationship with Solving works precisely because it's not romantic. He sees her as intellectual equal and colleague, she sees him as genuine friend who doesn't want her body. This is novel and precious to her.

Magical Approach

Laboratory-focused: Painful Magic and Slow Caster mean she prefers theoretical work to field casting. She's meticulous in lab, budgets time for research, and develops carefully optimized spells.

Single powerful effects: In combat, she favors one devastating spell rather than multiple small ones. Waves of Drowning, Catapult of Mighty Winds, or (in extremis) Kiss of Death. Each spell costs her physically; she makes them count.

Healer-Destroyer duality: Knows anatomy well enough to save OR kill. Medicine 4 represents her healing side, but her combat spells show she understands destruction equally well. She's Flambeau, after all - just an Orator variant.

Underdeveloped maritime specialization: She has the theoretical foundation (astronomy, navigation, weather prediction) but hasn't yet learned the practical spells for maritime work. Her current repertoire is combat-focused (what Rodrigo taught) rather than navigation-focused (what she'll develop). This is 1205 - she's about to start life at sea and will need to expand her capabilities.

Current Motivations (1205)

Survival and Mobility: Don't get trapped again. Keep moving. Be ready to flee. The North Sea ship-based covenant suits this perfectly - home is mobile, escape is built-in.

Helping Other Outcasts: She's drawn to Solving partly because he's also a misfit (Criamon mystic with impossible visions) and to Sjórseiðr because it's a covenant of refugees and outcasts. She wants to protect people who, like her, don't fit anywhere else.

Developing Maritime Magic: She needs weather-working, navigation, and ship-protection spells. This is practical necessity but also professional identity - she's positioning herself as the maritime specialist Sjórseiðr needs.

Finding Peace: The "eye of the hurricane" metaphor is literal. She finds stillness only by accepting chaos as permanent condition. She's seeking that equilibrium - not trying to stop being tempestuous, but learning to exist peacefully within the storm.

No Grand Plans: Unlike Solving's vision-driven quest, Khalida has no ultimate goal. She's surviving day-to-day, protecting people she cares about, and trying to find moments of stillness between disasters. If anything larger emerges, it'll be organic, not predetermined.

Relationship with Solving

Colleagues and friends bonded by shared trauma. He saved her life with his premonition; she validated his visions by trusting them. They debate Avicenna, discuss mysticism vs. rationality, and respect each other's intellectual traditions without requiring conversion.

She doesn't fully understand his Criamon mysticism, but she trusts his sincerity. He doesn't share her Islam, but he respects her faith's legitimacy. This mutual respect without requiring sameness is rare in their experience.

She's not invested in his vision of finding the North Sea vis source - that's his quest, not hers. But she'll help him pursue it because he's her friend and they're in this together. When his quest inevitably brings disaster (she knows from experience that obsessions lead to destruction), she'll try to protect him from the fallout.

Key NPCs and Unresolved Threads

Rodrigo de Tarsus (Parens): Fate unknown. Did he escape Al-Quṣṣāṣ? Return to Christian lands? Die in the destruction? Khalida doesn't know and is afraid to find out. Finding him alive would be joyous; confirming his death would be devastating; uncertainty is agonizing but safer than bad news.

Ibrahim ibn-Yusuf al-Balansī: Order of Suleiman mentor from Valencia. Presumably dead or fled, but could resurface as ally or as someone bearing news about Al-Quṣṣāṣ's fate.

Other Al-Quṣṣāṣ Survivors: How many escaped? Where are they? Do any hold her responsible for the destruction (survivor's guilt is common)? Could become allies, antagonists, or sources of painful memories.

Almohad Authorities: If they learn a "heretical sorceress" from Al-Quṣṣāṣ survived, they might send pursuit. Religious zealotry doesn't recognize borders.

The Order of Suleiman: Non-Hermetic Islamic magi might view Khalida as traitor for fully joining the Order of Hermes, or as potential bridge between traditions, or as dangerous hybrid who belongs to neither world.

Notes for Gamemaster

Painful Magic in Play: Each spell = one fatigue level of pain. This is cumulative. After 2-3 spells in combat, she's at serious penalties (-3 or worse). She'll avoid spamming spells and plan carefully. Out of combat, she can rest and recover normally.

Slow Caster in Combat: Two-round cast time means enemies get extra actions. She's vulnerable while casting. This encourages her to:

  • Use cover/protection while casting
  • Rely on allies for defense
  • Pick single decisive effects rather than multiple spells
  • Avoid combat when possible (Orator diplomacy)

Kiss of Death: Only used when physically cornered/grabbed. Casting it leaves her nearly incapacitated (PeCo 45 = massive pain). It's escape tool, not combat tactic.

Religious Practice: She performs salat five times daily and observes dietary restrictions. Usually this is background detail, but it can create story moments:

  • Interrupting for prayer during emergencies (Pious flaw)
  • Finding clean water for ablutions while traveling
  • Other characters' reactions to her faith (tolerance, curiosity, prejudice)
  • Debates about theology and magic

Medicine Skills: She's legitimately skilled healer (Medicine 4, Chirurgy 3, Affinity). NPCs and companions can come to her for treatment. This creates relationships and obligations beyond her magical power.

Curse of Venus: Should generate complications, not just harassment. Ideas:

  • Someone inappropriate falls for her (married magus, enemy's son, incompatible personality)
  • She develops crush on worst possible person at worst possible time
  • People she actually wants misinterpret her interest as vanity
  • Unwanted suitors create political complications

Cultural Tensions: As Moorish Muslim woman in Christian-dominated tribunal, she faces:

  • Casual prejudice and exoticization
  • Assumptions about her based on Reconquista propaganda
  • Difficulty being taken seriously
  • Suspicion from Church authorities
  • But also: curiosity, respect for her learning, recognition that the Gift transcends mundane politics

Tempestuous Personality: Mechanically, +3 means strong bonus in situations where intense emotion helps (intimidation, passionate arguments, weather magic reflecting mood). But it should also create problems: offending people, making impulsive decisions, letting anger overcome judgment.

Self-Protective (-2): She takes risks others wouldn't. Walks into danger. Volunteers for hazardous tasks. This isn't stupidity - she's intelligent and capable - but she doesn't value her own safety appropriately. Allies will need to protect her from herself.

Restlessness: She's uncomfortable with long-term plans or commitments. "Where will we be in five years?" type questions make her anxious. She thinks in seasons and short-term goals, not decades.

Growth Arc: She's in early stage of becoming the maritime specialist she's described as in notes. She has foundation but needs to develop:

  • Weather prediction spells (Sailor's Foretaste of the Morrow, extended duration weather sensing)
  • Wind control for navigation (Rego Auram effects for guiding ships)
  • Ship protection wards (weather wards, lightning protection)
  • Flight magic (Wings of the Soaring Wind)
  • Lightning control (offensive and defensive)

Regio and Aura Sensitivity: Her Magic Sensitivity 4 will prove invaluable in maritime environment. Vis sources and magical auras at sea are notoriously ephemeral and difficult to locate - appearing with specific tide conditions, moon phases, weather patterns, or seasonal confluences, then vanishing. Her ability to sense magical auras (Ease Factor 9 for level 3 aura, 6 for level 6 aura) means she can:

  • Detect temporary regiones that appear during storms or celestial events
  • Locate transient vis sources that only manifest under specific conditions
  • Sense magical creatures or phenomena before they become visible
  • Navigate to areas of higher magical concentration

This makes her reconnaissance specialist as well as weather-worker. Combined with her astronomical knowledge and weather prediction abilities, she can potentially predict when and where ephemeral magical phenomena will appear - "The convergence of storm, full moon, and spring tide will create a temporary regio at these coordinates." This transforms her from passive detector to active magical resource scout.

However, her Magic Sensitivity also subtracts from her Magic Resistance (score 4 = -4 to resistance), making her more vulnerable to magical attacks. This reinforces her preference for avoiding magical combat when possible.

multipleCasting.sumary: Int + Finesse + stress die - no of spells VS 9

basicLabTotal.sumary: Int + Magic theory + Aura (+ Technique + Form)

totalPenetration.sumary: Penetration + (Active effects penetration like Arcane connection or Sympathetic Connection)

sigil.label: Wizard's Sigil
sigil.value: The scent of ozone and a sudden, momentary drop in air pressure.

system.description:
This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

Knowledge of one particular region, covenant, or even a village. It includes knowing where things are in the immediate area, local history and legends, and the centers of power in the region. The smaller the region, the more detailed your knowledge.

Specialties: geography, history, legends, politics, personalities. (General)

Noticing things, be they things you are looking for or things that you are not expecting. It is also used to see how alert you are in circumstances that require watchfulness.

Specialties: bodyguarding, keeping watch, alertness, searching. (General)

The know-how to get the greatest return for a service or product, while paying the least. It involves reading the person with whom you haggle, a general knowledge of the value of goods, and presenting yourself in certain ways. A good haggler can easily overcome resistance in an inexperienced customer and see through attempts to over-value most merchandise.

Specialties: specific types of products, hard sell. (General)

Enticing, fascinating, and endearing others to you, but only on a personal basis. It can be used to win someone over emotionally, especially members of the opposite sex.

Specialties: courtly love, first impressions, being witty. (General)

This is Middle Ages-style surgery, used to help others recover from physical damage. It encompasses tending and binding wounds of all varieties, and the necessary but brutal skill of cauterization. See page @@ for rules

Specialties: cauterization, diagnosis, binding wounds, setting bones. (General)

Focusing your mental faculties on one task, particularly for extended periods of time. If you are attempting a feat that demands your extra attention, or if you have just failed an action and are trying again, the storyguide can call for a Concentration roll before you can make the attempt. This Ability is especially important for magi because it helps them maintain concentration on spells despite distractions.

Specialties: spell concentration, reading, lab work. (General)

Understanding the background, personality, and motives of another person. Often the storyguide secretly rolls a die when this Ability is used. Thus, you do not know if your character guessed correctly, or even botched.

Specialties: peasants, townsfolk, nobles, clergy, magi, the opposite sex. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

Fluency in a particular living language. Rather than a die roll modifier, your score in this Knowledge measures your ability to communicate.

Score

Fluency

Description

0

Point and grunt

With one or more experience points, you know “please”, ”thank you”, and a few other words.

1

Basic questions and answers

“Where is the church?,” “Do you sell food?” Constant mistakes, and an atrocious accent. People must speak slowly and often repeat themselves, and you cannot string a conversation together.

2

Basic conversation

You can sustain a short conversation on a common topic. You still make many mistakes, and often fail to catch what others say.

3

Haltingly functional

You can hold a conversation on everyday topics, although it takes time, you make many mistakes, and your accent is still bad.

4

Functional

You can hold a conversation on non-technical topics, and make few mistakes. People do not normally need to repeat themselves. This is the minimum level required to study from a book.

5

Fluent

You still have an accent if this is not your native language, but it is weak. You speak as well as most natives. This is the minimum level required to write a book.

6

Elegant

You choose your words well, and have no accent if this is not your native language.

Two dialects of the same language generally have a –1 penalty. If your character is not specialized in a dialect, you should specify a dialect without specializing in it, and take the penalty when conversing with people who speak a different dialect. If you speak a "standard" version of the language, that is a dialect that differs from all other dialects.

When two people speak to each other, the lower Ability score determines how well they communicate. Characters who speak related languages can communicate at a penalty to their scores (assigned by the storyguide) depending on how closely related the two languages are.

Note that Latin and Hebrew are not living languages in most of Mythic Europe. However, characters who live in places where they are (such as some covenants) may learn this Ability without any special Virtues.

Characters with this Ability are illiterate unless they also have a score in Artes Liberales (page 62).

This Ability also covers artistic compositions in the language, and telling existing tales with verve and passion. Knowledge of stories is covered by the appropriate Lore Abilities.

Specialties: poetry, prose, specific dialects, expansive vocabulary, specific technical vocabulary, slang, storytelling. (General)

The ability to sing, play musical instruments, and compose new music.

Specialties: sing, compose, any one instrument. (General)

The ability to propel yourself through water quickly and efficiently.

Specialties: long distances, diving, underwater maneuvering. (General)

The ability to teach an Ability to someone else.

Specialties: a particular Ability, particular kinds of student. (General)

You are often able to identify a place or object as magical. However, your sensitivity makes you more susceptible to magical effects: subtract your Magic Sensitivity score from your Magic Resistance. When attempting to sense the magic nature of a place or object, the storyguide will assign an Ease Factor-simply roll a simple die + Perception + Magic Sensitivity.

Detecting a Magic aura would normally have an Ease Factor equal to 12 – aura level, so 9 for a level 3 aura and 6 for a level 6 aura.

For spells and enchanted items, an Ease Factor equal to 21 – magnitude of effect is a good guide. For enchanted items, use the magnitude of the most powerful effect in the item.

For magical creatures, 15 – one fifth of the creature's Magic Might is appropriate, so for a creature with a Magic Might of 25, the Ease Factor would be 10.

Specialties: auras, magical creatures, enchanted items, active spells. (Supernatural)

Knowledge of what magic is and how it works, used primarily in the laboratory. Magic Theory deals with the technical details of Hermetic magic; Magic Lore covers knowledge of magical things in general. Anyone can learn Magic Theory, if they have access to a teacher or book (normally represented by a Virtue), but it is little use to those without The Gift.

Specialties: inventing spells, enchanting items, a single Art. (Arcane)

Knowledge of magical creatures, areas, and traditions.

Specialties: creatures, magical traditions, regiones. (Arcane)

Getting your spell through the target's Magic Resistance. See page @@ for detailed Penetration rules.

Specialties: any one Art. (Arcane)

Protection from magic. This is a special ritual (not a ritual spell) that takes about two minutes to perform. It lets you add 5 times your Parma Magica score to your Magic Resistance until the next sunrise or sunset, whichever comes first. You may suppress your Parma Magica temporarily by concentrating; this is equivalent to sustaining a Concentration duration spell. (See page @@). Parma Magica does not require concentration while it is active.

You may also protect one other person for each point in Parma Magica, with their consent. You must touch each person to start the protection, and it lasts as long as at least one character can see the other. The magus may cancel the protection at will, at any distance. While a magus is protecting others, his effective Parma Magica score is reduced by 3 points, both for himself and for the other people he is protecting. If the magus has a Parma Magica score of 3 or lower, his Parma Magica provides each character, including himself, with a Magic Resistance of 0. In the magus's case, this is added to his Form resistance, and a magus protected by another magus's Parma Magica may also add his Form resistance.

Any character protected by a Parma Magica, whether their own or someone else’s, is unaffected by the social penalties of other people’s Gifts. If a protected character is Gifted, their Gift still imposes social penalties on others, unless those others are also protected by a Parma Magica, or are unaffected by The Gift for some other reason.

Parma Magica can only be learned by Gifted characters, although they learn it as a normal Arcane Ability, not a Supernatural Ability. It is only known by Hermetic magi, as the Order enforces the 'Join or Die' choice rigorously on anyone who knows it, as well as declaring a Wizard's March on the magus who taught it. Parma Magica is the last thing an apprentice learns, being taught the final key to the Ability after he swears the Oath.

Specialties: protection from any specific Form. (Arcane)

Knowledge of the manifestations of the power of the Divine. Different from Church Lore (a kind of (Organization) Lore)) because it covers miracles rather than politics, and different from Theology because it is concerned with power rather than doctrine.

Specialties: angels, saints, Divine creatures. (Arcane)

Judging events according to the Code of Hermes and the Peripheral Code. In addition to memorizing important precedents, this knowledge includes the practical side of enforcing Hermetic law - for example, knowing when to push for a vote or how to present an argument.

Specialties: Wizards' Marches, apprentices, mundane relations, Tribunal procedures, political intrigue. (Arcane)

Fighting at range using weapons which are thrown.

Specialties: any one weapon. (Martial)

This is the Islamic equivalent of Theology: Christian. The major sources for Islamic theology are the Qur’an and the Sunna.

Specialties: the Qur’an, debates, history, prophets. (Academic)

There are three philosophies — natural philosophy, moral philosophy, and metaphysics. Aristotle is the auctor for all of them, in different books. Most scholars study the philosophies after the Arts but before going on to Law, Theology or Medicine. Natural philosophy is the study of the sub-lunar world. It roughly corresponds to today's science. The main texts are the Physica, De Meteorologia, and De historia animalium. Moral philosophy is the study of the proper way of human life. It covers ethics, politics, and economics — the texts are the Ethica, Politica, and Economica. Metaphysics is the philosophy of the fundamental nature of the world. The main text is Aristotle's Metaphysica. The theories of the Philosophiae are all basically correct in Mythic Europe.

Specialties: natural philosophy, moral philosophy, metaphysics, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Medicine is the formal study of the body and its diseases. Medical practice is based on the theory of the four humors: blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile. Diseases are believed to be caused by imbalances among them, hence such practices as bloodletting, to restore the balance. Medicine also deals with treating poisons and the results of starvation and the like, but not with treating wounds (see "Chirurgy," above). The authorities are Galen and Hippocrates, in many works.

Specialties: anatomy, apothecary, physician. (Academic)

This skill is similar to all other (Language) Abilities, but it is only available to educated characters. It grants the ability to speak a language which is no longer used as a native language in the areas the character has lived in. The most important example is Latin. All educated characters in the West know Latin, since without it you cannot learn any Academic Knowledges. It is also the common language of the Church and Order of Hermes. In other areas of the world, Arabic, Greek and Hebrew fill similar functions, although of these only Hebrew is a dead language.

Specialties: academic usage, Church ceremonies, Hermetic usage. (Academic)

The seven artes liberales, or liberal arts, are the basis of medieval higher education. Everyone learns them at the universities before passing on to study other subjects. It would be very rare for a character to have a score in any other Academic Knowledge without a score of at least one in this.

The artes liberales are divided into two groups: the Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and the Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). The emphasis at this period is on the Trivium, but both are taught. (Note that Artes Liberales as a whole is one Skill, not seven separate ones.)

Grammar concerns theoretical questions of the structure of languages, rather than the ability to use a specific language (that is covered by Speak skills). The authorities are Priscian and Donatus. Logic is the study of the syllogism, and other forms of reasoning discussed by Aristotle. It is the most important of the arts at this period. Aristotle is the authority, in his logica vetus and logica nova. Rhetoric is the study of the theory of rhetorical forms: knowledge of the sort of tricks that an orator can use, rather than the ability to use them. The authority is Cicero, especially his De Inventione.

Arithmetic is the ability to add and subtract, multiply and divide. The authority is Boethius, De arithmetica. Geometry is concerned with study of plane and solid figures, and the authority is Euclid, in his Elementa. It also deals with the study of light, and Euclid's Optica is the authority here. Astronomy is concerned with predicting the positions of the stars and planets, and the authority is Ptolemy, in the Libri Almagesti. Music is purely theoretical, the study of proportions and musical theory, not the ability to sing or play an instrument. The authority is Boethius, in De Musica.

Artes Liberales also covers the ability to read and write. For every point in the Ability, the character can read and write one writing system, providing that he knows a language which normally uses that system: the Latin alphabet, the Greek alphabet, the Arabic alphabet, and so on.

Specialties: grammar, logic, rhetoric, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, music, Ritual magic, Ceremonial magic. (Academic)

Civil Law is the law of the Roman Empire. Most local legal systems are based upon it (England being the notable exception), and when a law is needed to govern international relations, Civil Law is appealed to. The authority is Justinian's Digest. Canon Law is the law of the Church. It is important to Mythic Europe as it applies in all nations, governing the working of the church and some other areas, such as marriage. It is important to note that members of the clergy are only subject to Canon Law, not customary or Civil Law. Most high churchmen are skilled canonists, not theologians. Canon Law is made by Papal bulls (pronouncements), but the authority is Gratian, in his Decretum. This skill covers the knowledge of both types of law. Other religions, particularly Judaism and Islam, have their own versions of this Ability, which must be learned separately.

Specialties: laws and customs of a specific area, papal laws. (Academic)

The Gift is a special Virtue, because it has no cost. The character suffers all the penalties of The Gift, just as magi do (see page @@), but can be taught Supernatural Abilities without having to take the corresponding Virtues (see page @@ for rules). Most importantly, the character can be taught Hermetic Magic, so all magi must have this Virtue. A character with The Gift, even if he is not a magus, may take Hermetic Virtues and Flaws which relate to intrinsic ability rather than background or training. Characters may take Virtues that grant Supernatural Abilities without taking The Gift, and such characters do not suffer the penalties imposed by The Gift. However, such characters may not learn new Supernatural Abilities in the course of the saga, although they may improve the ones they already have. It is possible for unGifted characters to Initiate new Supernatural Abilities through a Mystery Cult, but even this is harder for them than for the Gifted.

Characters who have The Gift may start play with a single Supernatural Ability, without having to take any other Virtue, but if they wish to learn others they must find opportunities to do so in the course of the saga. They may also take Virtues granting Supernatural Abilities if they wish to have more Abilities at character creation. Note that it is harder for a character with Supernatural Abilities to become a Hermetic magus (see Training Your Apprentice on page @@), so you may not wish to take any such abilities if you plan for the character to become an apprentice. The ability to cast Hermetic magic is the single supernatural ability possessed by Hermetic magi in virtue of The Gift again, they may take more Supernatural Ability Virtues if they wish.

Grogs can never have The Gift, as a character with The Gift is too important to be a grog. As a rule, companions should only have The Gift if they are intended to become magi, or if they are members of one of the other magical traditions described in Ars Magica 5th Edition supplements. Troupes should not allow any other Gifted characters unless they are absolutely sure that they want them.

You add 3 to the value of one Art whenever you use it. This means all totals in which the score of the Art is part of the total. It does not apply when learning, teaching, or writing about the Art. You may take this Virtue twice, for two different Arts. If a spell has requisites, include the bonus from Puissant Art with that Art when calculating which Art is higher. If the Puissant Art is higher, the bonus does not apply to the requisite.

Your parens was more powerful, or a better teacher, than is common. You gain an additional 60 experience points and 30 spell levels during apprenticeship. This Virtue has no effect on your study after apprenticeship, but you still have a close relationship with a powerful magus.

You are often able to identify a place or object as magical. However, your sensitivity makes you more susceptible to magical effects: subtract your Magic Sensitivity score from your Magic Resistance. Choosing this Virtue confers the Ability Magic Sensitivity 1 (page @@).
You are a member of the Order of Hermes. All magi must take this as their Social Status, and only magi may take it.
Through piety and holy devotion you have faith that can move mountains. You have a True Faith score of one and can gain more. For more about True Faith, see page @@.

All Advancement Totals for one Ability are increased by half, rounded up, as are any experience points you put in that Ability at character creation. You may only take this Virtue once for a given Ability, but may take it again for different Abilities. If you take this Virtue for an Ability, you may exceed the normal age-based cap during character generation (see page @@) by two points for that Ability.
You have a talent for comprehending the writings of others. When studying from books, treat them as if they were three Quality levels higher than they actually are.

You are very careful with a specific Ability, and are less likely to fail spectacularly if you do fail when using it. You roll two fewer botch dice than normal whenever you are required to roll botch dice for that Ability. This may mean that you roll no botch dice. This Virtue may apply to any Ability, even one you cannot learn at character creation.

You have been educated in a mosque or Qur’an school, and may purchase Academic Abilities during character generation. You get an extra 50 experience points at character creation, which must be spent on some or all of Arabic, Persian, Greek, Latin, Theology: Islam, Islamic Law, and Artes Liberales.

You are particularly adept with one Ability, and add 2 to its value whenever you use it. Note that you do not, in general, use an Ability when learning it, writing about it, or helping someone else to improve it. You may only take this Virtue once for a given Ability, but may take it more than once for different Abilities.

Casting spells causes you to suffer the equivalent of one Fatigue level in pain for each spell you cast. This reduces all your actions by the appropriate Fatigue penalty, which is cumulative with any from actual fatigue or injuries (though you do not suffer any physical damage from pain). You recover these "pain levels" just like Fatigue levels.

For some reason you are completely unable to use two combinations of Techniques and Forms. For example, you may be unable to use Intellego Herbam and Intellego Animal. You may not use these Arts together even if one or both are requisites. This Flaw may be taken repeatedly with different combinations, but may not be combined with a Deficiency (see page @@).

Your magic requires more time to prepare and execute than that of other magi. Your Formulaic spells take two rounds to cast; Spontaneous spells also take two rounds unless you fast-cast, in which case they take one round casting time. Fast-cast Mastered spells also take the normal one-round time. You can still cast Muto Vim spells on your own spells, but the casting process takes longer. Ritual spells and ceremonial castings are performed as normal, since all magi must cast them slowly and carefully.

You have the rough task of making it on your own-normal society rejects you and you are not attached to a covenant. Perhaps you have a magical nature, a supernatural background, some disfigurement, or a tremendous scandal in your past. You may not take the Wealthy Virtue.

You are very attractive to people whom you do not wish to attract. People you detest keep getting crushes on you, and will not be dissuaded. Furthermore, you tend to fall in love with inappropriate people, and in inappropriate circumstances. Those people you are interested in tend to think you are vain and shallow.
You are a fervent follower of your religion. You avoid the things it prohibits, and enthusiastically do the things it commands.

Raises a wave 5 feet high and 30 paces wide, which can submerge swimmers, overturn rowboats, and damage sailing ships. It starts as a small, curved wave and grows for the first 10 paces, at which point it reaches maximum size. If it travels 100 paces without striking an object large enough to break it up, it dissipates.

Terrifically strong winds sweep up an object (such as a barrel, a piece of furniture, or an unfortunate human being) and hurl it on a high, arched trajectory toward any point within range. If the projectile has Magic Resistance, this spell must penetrate in order to affect it. However, once the projectile is airborne, its motion is natural and hence it bypasses the Magic Resistance of anything it hits. You must succeed at an aiming roll to hit the intended target. Because this spell gives the caster only indirect control of the projectile, the aiming roll suffers a -3 penalty and an extra botch die. The winds are strong enough to throw a grown man 20 feet into the air. Both the projectile and anything underneath it suffer damage when it crashes to earth: the damage is usually +10 but it could be less if the projectile is considerably lighter than an adult human.

Converts clean air into foul humors that can cause disease. The humors form a cloud about 20 paces across and ten paces high. They are invisible but foul-smelling and noxious. Characters who breathe the befouled air must make Stamina roll against an Ease Factor of 6 or contract malaria (roll once, the first time a character is exposed). This disease, whose name is Italian for “bad air,” causes a Medium Wound. Once a character has contracted the disease, further exposure to this spell has no effect. The foul humors dissipate when the spell Duration expires. A brisk breeze can disperse the humors within two or three rounds, and strong winds make the spell unusable.

Extinguish any fire up to the size of a house fire. Many Flambeau magi learn this spell, or carry items enchanted with a similar effect, to control any incidental fires caused by errant Creo Ignem magic.

The person you kiss dies a sudden death. The only mark on the corpse is the black imprint of your lips. You do not need to speak when casting this spell, and thus gain no penalty from silence. (This is why the spell is higher level than the guidelines would indicate.)

Manipulating your spells and performing special feats with them. You would use this Ability to position objects delicately or to target spells.

Specialties: grace, precision, any one Form. (Arcane)
